coredata.dc:description: To improve the photocatalytic performance, carbon nitride (C3N4) heterojunctions with carbon nanomaterials are being developed. Here, the electrostatic self-assembly method was used to create the GOQDs (graphene oxide quantum dots)/Hp-C3N4 (HNO3 protonated carbon nitride) heterojunctions. Poor dispersion and weak interface bonding were addressed by using GOQDs, a derivative of graphene oxide (GO). The specific surface area of the GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 heterojunction increased from 7.445 m2/g to 33.634 m2/g, and the valence band potential increased from 2.016 eV to 2.367 eV. The GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 could generate higher levels of •OH and superoxide radicals (•O2 -). The degradation efficiency of the heterojunction for pollutants (MO) under visible light was increased by 343, 9, and 4 times in comparison to C3N4, Hp-C3N4, and GO/Hp- C3N4, respectively. Our results showed that GOQDs derived from GO with smaller sizes and higher abundances of oxygen-containing functional groups make better co-catalysts for constructing heterojunctions with C3N4 to boost photocatalytic performance.

The mechanism of enhanced photocatalytic performance of GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 heterojunction.

head.ce:title: Constructing a perfect, efficient heterojunction catalyst with HNO3 protonated C3N4 and GO-derived GOQDs via electrostatic self-assembly

ce:author.ce:contributor-role: Funding acquisition, Investigation, Writing – reviewing and editing, Funding acquisition, Investigation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To improve the photocatalytic performance, carbon nitride (C3N4) heterojunctions with carbon nanomaterials are being developed. Here, the electrostatic self-assembly method was used to create the GOQDs (graphene oxide quantum dots)/Hp-C3N4 (HNO3 protonated carbon nitride) heterojunctions. Poor dispersion and weak interface bonding were addressed by using GOQDs, a derivative of graphene oxide (GO). The specific surface area of the GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 heterojunction increased from 7.445 m2/g to 33.634 m2/g, and the valence band potential increased from 2.016 eV to 2.367 eV. The GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 could generate higher levels of •OH and superoxide radicals (•O2 -). The degradation efficiency of the heterojunction for pollutants (MO) under visible light was increased by 343, 9, and 4 times in comparison to C3N4, Hp-C3N4, and GO/Hp- C3N4, respectively. Our results showed that GOQDs derived from GO with smaller sizes and higher abundances of oxygen-containing functional groups make better co-catalysts for constructing heterojunctions with C3N4 to boost photocatalytic performance.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: As a new metal-free polymeric semiconductor photocatalyst, carbon nitride has drawn a lot of interest due to its excellent physicochemical stability [1], non-toxicity [2], ease of preparation [3], and suitable bandgap (2.7–2.9 eV) [4]. Though successful applications of carbon-nitride-based photocatalysts for hydrogen evolution [5], CO2 reduction [6], and pollutant degradation [7] have been made, they can only be used in a small number of applications due to their small specific surface area [8], low visible light absorption [9], and quick photogenerated carrier recombination [10].

In order to increase the photocatalytic activity of carbon nitride in visible light, heterojunctions with carbon nitride have been constructed using carbon nanomaterials such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene, and graphene oxide (GO) [11]. This has been done by increasing light absorption and photogenerated carrier transport. For instance, Zhao et al. [12] prepared hydroxylated carbon nanotube-modified carbon nitride nanoribbons (HCNT/CNN) by combining solvothermal and sonication, and the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) was increased by 4 times compared to that of CNN. Pu et al. [13] created reduced graphene oxide modified porous protonated graphitic carbon nitride (pGCN-rGO), whose photocatalytic degradation rate of methylene blue (MB) was increased by 4-fold and 2-fold compared to GCN and pGCN, respectively. By treating pure g-C3N4 with HNO3, Xie et al. [14] were able to produce protonated carbon nitride (pCN). They then created pCN/GO composites by having GO and pCN self-assemble through the synergistic effect of ultrasonic stripping and electrostatic attraction. The degradation efficiency of GO/pCN to ciprofloxacin and Rhodamine B (RhB) under visible light improved greatly. Gu et al. [15] prepared g-C3N4/rGO nanocomposites using a hydrothermal technique assisted by alkaline water. The separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers can be increased by using g-C3N4/rGO nanocomposites because they have a higher specific surface area and rGO can be used as a good electron acceptor and conductive channel. In comparison to g-C3N4, g-C3N4/rGO exhibits 2.7 times more photocatalytic denitrification activity.

Due to limitations in the microstructure and characteristics of carbon nanomaterial, the photocatalytic performance has somewhat improved but not to the extent anticipated. For instance, CNTs are prone to aggregation due to their extremely high aspect ratio and strong van der Waals force [16,17], and it is difficult to achieve a stable interface bond and uniform dispersion with the carbon nitride matrix. Graphene has a typical sheet-like structure with a few functional groups on the surface. Since single-layer graphene exhibits a strong van der Waals force, its dispersion is typically poor [18,19]. Even though the GO surface has some functional groups that contain oxygen to improve its dispersion, the large lateral size (micron level) always restricts the improvement of GO dispersion stability, and the large lamellar structure makes it simple to block the photocatalytic reaction sites of carbon nitride when GO and carbon nitride form heterojunctions. This makes CNTs, graphene, and GO easily aggregate on the surface or in the pores of the material when making heterojunctions with carbon nitride, which may reduce the substrate's specific surface area and make it difficult for them to form stable interfacial bonds and uniform dispersion. As a result, the photocatalytic performance of the created heterojunctions is only slightly improved or their cycling stability is decreased.

There is hope for addressing the issues of poor bonding and poor dispersion of carbon materials (CNTs, graphene, GO, and carbon nitride) because graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs), which are derived from GO, have excellent dispersion because of their extremely small nano size and rich oxygen-containing functional groups [20]. As the name suggests, GOQDs are single-layer or few-layer graphene with lateral dimensions of less than 20 nm and a lot of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface, combining the majority of the exceptional qualities and characteristics of both GO and QDs [21], such as excellent electron transfer ability [22], efficient spectral absorption [23], unique photo-induced up-conversion effect [24], and obvious quantum dot confinement effect and edge effect [25,26]. In particular, the particular shape, extremely small nanometer size, and abundant edges make GOQDs have a higher ability to penetrate into pores and smaller spaces, and the abundance of oxygen-containing groups on GOQDs makes them easy to disperse and can form more solid interfacial bonding and uniform distribution with the substrate catalyst without agglomeration [27]. GOQDs are a latecomer among graphene-based nanomaterials, and they can be made by specifically altering GO [28]. However, little research has been reported on the performance of GOQDs and GO in photocatalysis. To build heterojunctions with carbon nitride to improve the photocatalytic performance of carbon nitride, both GO and GOQDs can be used as co-catalysts in photocatalysis. But, no research has been reported on which co-catalyst is superior between GO and GOQDs.

In earlier reports, co-calcination of graphene-based nanomaterials and carbon nitride precursors (melamine, dicyandiamide, urea, etc.) was typically used to prepare composites of graphene-based nanomaterials and carbon nitride. The surface functional groups (carboxyl and carbonyl groups) of graphene-based nanomaterials are easily decomposed at high temperatures of 500–600 °C [30,31], and the composites produced by solid-phase reactions at high temperatures are still blocky [29], leading to a small specific surface area and few active sites in the heterojunction. The structure and surface of graphene-based nanomaterials are inevitably harmed by the gases released during thermal polymerization, and the electrical conductivity and separation of electrons and holes are also negatively impacted [32,33]. Besides, the hydrothermal method was also used to create composites of graphene-based nanomaterials and carbon nitride, but this typically necessitates expensive machinery like high-pressure reactors and is constrained by the reaction vessel, making it unsuitable for mass production. As a result, it is still very difficult to create carbon nitride and graphene-based nanomaterial heterojunctions with effective photocatalytic performance [34].

This paper reports the performance disparity between GO and GOQDs in the area of photocatalysis. GOQDs were created by cleaving GO with concentrated HNO3, and both GO and GOQDs underwent thorough characterization and analysis. Thermal polymerization of melamine produced the initial carbon nitride (C3N4), while concentrated HNO3 treatment of C3N4 produced the positively charged protonated carbon nitride (Hp-C3N4), which was then used to create GO/Hp-C3N4 and GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 heterojunctions through electrostatic self-assembly. Due to their smaller size and higher abundance of oxygen-containing functional groups when compared to GO, GOQDs derived from GO are a more superior and ideal co-catalyst, according to the experimental results and characterization analysis. This is, as far as we are aware, the first report on the distinction between GO and GOQDs for use in carbon nitride matrix photocatalysts. Additionally, the prepared GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 heterojunction accomplishes the triple composite modification of morphology modification, energy band modulation, and construction of heterojunction for carbon nitride at the same time, making the GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 heterojunction with effective photocatalytic activity. This study offers a fresh strategy for improving the photocatalytic applications of nanomaterials based on graphene.

Melamine, graphene oxide (GO), concentrated HNO3 (64–68%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), methyl orange (MO), p-benzoquinone (PBQ), isopropyl alcohol (IPQ), and sodium oxalate (Na2C2O4) were all of analytical grade and did not require further purification. GO was purchased from Chengdu Organic Chemical Co., Ltd., Chinese Academy of Sciences. Ultrapure water (>18.5 MΩ cm−1 resistivity) was used throughout the experiments.

By using a single concentrated HNO3 as a potent oxidant, GOQDs were created by cleaving GO. First, 0.5 g of GO was combined with 250 mL of concentrated HNO3 in a 500 mL round-bottom flask. The mixture was then heated and stirred at 140 °C for 60 h (with a condensing reflux device), and the solution gradually turned from black to reddish-brown and then to clear yellow. The solution was then cooled to room temperature, and the majority of the concentrated HNO3 was recovered using a rotary evaporator. After being neutralized to a pH of 6.0–8.0 in an ice bath with NaOH solution, the solution underwent dialysis for three to five days using ultrapure water to remove salt impurities. GOQDs were finally obtained by freeze-drying the material for 36 h.

C3N4 was created by thermally polymerizing melamine. 15.0 g of melamine were placed in a crucible with a lid, heated to 550 °C in a muffle furnace at a heating rate of 2.1 °C/min, and held for 4 h to create a yellow block. This block was then cooled to room temperature and ground into a homogeneous powder in a mortar, which was designated as C3N4.

When 5.0 g of the previously prepared C3N4 was combined with 200 mL of concentrated HNO3 in a round-bottom flask and refluxed at 80 °C for 3 h, the solution turned from yellow to milky white. The solution was heated to 60 °C for 24 h, cooled to room temperature, filtered, and repeatedly washed with ultrapure water to remove any remaining HNO3, and then ground to create a white powder that was designated as Hp-C3N4.

The electrostatic self-assembly process was used to construct the GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 heterojunction. 1.0 g of Hp-C3N4 and 10 mg of GOQDs were added to 100 mL of ultrapure water, and the mixture was stirred for 6 h. The heterojunction of GOQDs and Hp-C3N4 was produced after centrifugation and drying, which is labeled as GOQDs/Hp-C3N4. In order to be compared to GO, the GO/Hp-C3N4 heterojunction was created using the same method. It was then designated as GO/Hp-C3N4. In order to investigate the effects of various GOQD loadings on the photocatalytic performance, GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 with GOQD loading mass fractions of 0.50 %, 0.75 %, 1.0 %, 1.25 %, and 1.50 % were prepared by the same process. These samples were assigned the following labels, in order: 0.50 %, 0.75 %, 1.0 %, 1.25 %, and 1.50 % GOQDs/Hp-C3N4.

The photocatalytic performance of the catalysts was evaluated using the MO degradation under visible light. To produce visible light, a filter (>400 nm) and a 300 W xenon lamp were used. For each test, a photochemical reactor containing 80 mL of aqueous MO solution (10 ppm) and 80 mg of catalyst were weighed, added, and stirred in the dark for 30 min to achieve adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Every 15 min, 5 mL of the sample was taken, centrifuged to remove the supernatant, and the attenuation of the supernatant's absorbance at wavelength 460 nm was measured by UV–vis, which translates to the attenuation of the pollutant concentration.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Nova Namo-450, FEI) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2-TF30, S-Twin) were used to analyze the microscopic morphology and structure of the samples. The phases were determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD; RINT, Rigaku, Japan) with Cu Kα radiation. Surface groups, valence and chemical composition of samples were analyzed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet is 10) and X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS, K-Alpha+, Thermo Fisher Scientific). The dispersion stability of samples and the surface charge were analyzed using HORIBA SZ-100 analyzers. The optical properties of samples were tested by a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-3600 plus, Japan) and a fluorescence spectrometer (FLS1000). The transient photocurrent response (TPR) of samples was tested by an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E). The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area was measured using ASAP2020HD88. Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra were detected using a Bruker model ESR JES-FA200 spectrometer.

In Scheme1, the process for converting GO into GOQDs is described. First of all, because the surface of the precursor GO is primarily made up of oxygen-containing functional groups like hydroxyl, with a small amount of carboxyl and carbonyl, concentrated HNO3 will preferentially attack the initial defects of GO to generate more defect sites. The C-C/CC bond of GO is gradually broken and opened by the cleavage of concentrated HNO3 at the new defect sites. Furthermore, the concentrated HNO3 will strongly oxidize, converting the majority of the hydroxyl groups into carbonyl and carboxyl groups. Finally, all of the large-sized GO was divided into small-sized GOQDs.

The TEM images of GO and GOQDs are shown in Fig. 1a and b, respectively. After oxidative cleavage of concentrated HNO3 at high temperature, two-dimensional GO (micro-scale) was successfully cleaved into zero-dimensional GOQDs (nano-scale). GOQDs have a well-distributed particle size with a range of 1.0–7.0 nm and an average particle size of 3.68 nm (inset in Fig. 1b). The GOQDs have good crystallinity with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which can be attributed to the typical graphene (100) diffraction plane, as shown by the high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 1c). Fig. 1d shows the comparison of the XRD patterns of GO and GOQDs. GO has a sharp peak at 2θ = 10.3°, which is a typical diffraction peak of GO, corresponding to the (001) diffraction plane of graphene. After cleavage by concentrated HNO3, the GOQDs only have an obvious broad peak around 2θ = 26.2°, which is attributed to the graphene (002 diffraction plane. We successfully cleaved GO to create GOQDs, according to TEM and XRD analysis.

The UV–vis spectra of GOQDs exhibit two distinct absorption peaks at 266 nm and 347 nm (see Fig. 1e), where the former peak is associated with the aromatic sp2 (CC) π-π * transition and the latter peak is associated with the n-π * sp3 (CO) transitions [35]. The inset shows the good dispersion of the GOQDs. The Tyndall effect indicates the presence of nano-colloidal particles (GOQDs) and the GOQDs emit green fluorescence under 365 nm UV light irradiation, which is consistent with the PL spectral analysis. In addition, as shown in Fig. 1f, the PL spectra show that GOQDs could emit 520 nm-wavelength light under long-wavelength (460–900 nm) excitation, and this up-conversion effect shows that GOQDs could absorb and utilize more visible light. This is advantageous for enhancing the absorption and utilization of visible light by the heterojunction to improve the photocatalytic performance. And the emission wavelengths are all stable at around 520 nm, and this excitation independence can be attributed to the uniform size and high purity of GOQDs [36,37].

Fig. 2a shows the FT-IR spectra of GO and GOQDs. For GO, the stretching vibrations of O-H, CC, and C-O are represented by absorption peaks at approximately 3400, 1600, and 1420 cm−1, respectively. In addition, GO exhibits a weak peak at a wavelength of 1725 cm−1, which is consistent with the presence of a few carbonyl groups (CO) on the GO surface. For GOQDs, the carbonyl group's (CO) absorption peak's intensity is significantly increased near 1725 cm−1, and two new peaks appear at 870 cm−1 and 1250 cm−1, which correspond to the out-of-plane and in-plane bending vibrations of C-H, respectively. According to FT-IR analysis results, the surface of GOQDs has a large number of abundant functional groups.

To learn more about the chemical make-up and bonding structure of GO and GOQDs, XPS spectra were used. As shown in Fig. 2b, both GO and GOQDs are mainly composed of C and O elements, and the relative atomic percentages of C and O in GO are 66.36% and 31.59%, respectively. The C and O contents of GOQDs are 56.24 % and 41.5 %, respectively. Fig. 2c shows the high-resolution C1s spectra of GO and GOQDs. The three peaks at 284.8, 286.4, and 288.5 eV correspond to CC/C-C, C-O, and CO (carbonyl group), respectively. Fig. 2d shows the O1s high-resolution spectra of GO and GOQDs. The carbonyl group (CO), carboxylic acid (C-O), and oxygen (OC-O) are represented by the three peaks at 531.8, 532.6, and 533.4 eV, respectively. The FT-IR spectral analysis clearly shows that the carboxyl and carbonyl groups on the surface of GOQDs are much more abundant than those on GO, and this is consistent with the XPS analysis results. The strong oxidation of concentrated HNO3 is the main reason for the presence of these oxygen-containing functional groups in greater abundance on GOQDs' surface.

The large π-bonds within the graphene layers are broken up by the introduction of numerous oxygen-containing functional groups, which can enhance the dispersion stability of the layers by weakening the strong van der Waals between them. Furthermore, the dispersibility of GOQDs is further improved by their extremely small (3.56 nm) nanometer size. Excellent GOQD dispersion is conferred by the extremely small nanosized and the abundance of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface, which creates a supportive environment for the subsequent construction of heterojunctions with Hp-C3N4.

The C3N4 produced by direct thermal polymerization of melamine, as shown in Fig. 3a, primarily exhibits two-dimensional lamellae with a large size and smooth surface, which is not advantageous for the absorption and utilization of light [38]. The Hp-C3N4 produced by concentrated HNO3 modification treatment is fragmented (see Fig. 3b), which is advantageous to increase specific surface area to increase the photocatalytic reaction active sites and enhance photocatalytic performance. Fig. 3c is the SEM image of GOQDs/Hp- C3N4. Due to the GOQDs' incredibly small size (3.5 nm), the loading of GOQDs on the surface of Hp-C3N4 cannot be seen in the SEM image, but the presence of pores can be seen in the high-magnification SEM image of GOQDs/Hp- C3N4 (see Fig. 3d).

TEM was used to visually demonstrate the successful bonding of GOQDs with Hp- C3N4. The typical two-dimensional lamellar structure of C3N4 is shown in Fig. 4a with a thick thickness, whereas the Hp-C3N4 (Fig. 4b) becomes thin and the distribution of pores can be seen, which is consistent with the SEM analysis. Only a small amount of GO is loaded on the edge of Hp- C3N4, as shown in Fig. 4c, which may be due to GO's poor dispersion. Fig. 4d shows that the GOQDs particles are uniformly loaded on the surface of Hp-C3N4. The HR-TEM (Fig. 4d inset) reveals that the lattice spacing of GOQDs is 0.21 nm, which is consistent with the previous analysis (see Fig. 1c). The efficient photocatalytic activity of GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 heterojunction may be primarily due to the extremely small nano-size of GOQDs and great dispersion, which enables GOQDs to create a good interfacial connection with Hp-C3N4 [39].

The photocatalytic performance can be enhanced by the large specific surface area, which can increase the photocatalytic reaction site. The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm curve was used to examine the specific surface area and pore structure of the catalysts. As shown in Fig. 5a, according to the standard classification of the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC), the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of C3N4, Hp-C3N4, and GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 show an obvious H3 hysteresis loop in the range of 0.5–1.0 P/P0, which indicates that the pores of the catalysts are mainly mesoporous (2–50 nm) in size. The specific surface area of C3N4 is smaller (BET = 7.137 m2/g). However, after concentrated HNO3 was protonated, the large amounts of C3N4 are exfoliated into tiny pieces of Hp-C3N4, giving Hp-C3N4 a larger specific surface area (BET = 33.442 m2/g).

According to earlier studies, the cocatalyst may block the matrix's pores when creating the heterojunction, slightly reducing the heterojunction's specific surface area [40]. However, due to the large specific surface area and good dispersion of the GOQDs themselves, the specific surface area of the GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 heterojunctions prepared in this work did not decrease (BET = 33.634 m2/g). The pore size distribution curves of C3N4, Hp- C3N4, and GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 are shown in Fig. 5b. In contrast to Hp-C3N4 and GOQDs/Hp-C3N4, the C3N4 pore size distribution is primarily 2–10 nm, and there are fewer pores overall. This is in line with the aforementioned SEM and TEM analyses, which demonstrate that the pores of the GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 heterojunction can range in size from 20 to 60 nm.

As can be seen in Fig. 5c, GO has a moderately poor dispersibility as indicated by its Zeta potential value of − 34.6 mV. The extremely small nanometer size and a wealth of hydrophilic oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of GOQDs are responsible for their extremely high zeta potential value of − 90.5 mV, which demonstrates excellent dispersibility and stability. C3N4 has a low dispersion and a negative Zeta potential of − 21.8 mV. After concentrated HNO3 was used to protonate C3N4, the resultant Hp-C3N4 had a Zeta potential of + 57.3 mV, which not only enabled the surface to change from negative to positive charge but also improved Hp-C3N4's dispersion stability. Moreover, because of their attractive binding, negatively charged GOQDs and positively charged Hp-C3N4 have a slightly lower zeta potential value than Hp-C3N4 (+39.2 mV). This also suggests indirectly that our electrostatic self-assembly method, which was used to create a GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 heterojunction, is successful in combining GOQDs and Hp-C3N4.

The impact of protonation treatment and electrostatic self-assembly on the crystal structure of carbon nitride was examined using XRD. The graphite-like structure (002) crystal plane of C3N4 has two distinctive XRD peaks, as shown in Fig. 5d: a strong peak near 2θ = 27.3° for the interlayer stacking, and a weak peak near 2θ = 13.2° for the in-plane structure-filling motif of the (100) crystal plane of the tri-s-triazine unit. The disappearance of the Hp-C3N4 (100) diffraction peak is caused by the thermal exfoliation of HNO3 [41], which disrupts the Hp-C3N4 tri-s-triazine unit's in-plane periodic arrangement and causes the Hp-C3N4 in-plane size to decrease [42], which is consistent with the SEM analysis. In addition, after protonation, the (002) diffraction peak position is shifted toward a large angle (Fig. 5e), which can be attributed to the introduction of oxygen defects by concentrated HNO3 protonation to strengthen the interaction between adjacent layers, resulting in the reduction of the layer spacing [43]. Due to GOQDs' trace loading, which prevents Hp-C3N4 from stacking its interlayers, the intensity of the (002) plane diffraction peak of GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 is significantly lower than that of Hp-C3N4. Additionally, due to the low content and uniform dispersion of GOQDs, the characteristic peaks of GOQDs were not visible in the GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 XRD patterns.

The FT-IR spectra of C3N4, Hp-C3N4, GO/Hp-C3N4, and GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 are displayed in Fig. 5f. The same typical absorption bands as carbon nitride are accessible to all catalysts. The absorption band at 808 cm−1 can be attributed to the typical stretching vibration of the tris-s-triazine building block. A number of prominent absorption bands at 1240, 1321, 1409, 1538, and 1629 cm−1 may be related to the stretching vibrations of C-N and CN heterocycles. The broad absorption band at 2900–3600 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of N-H/O-H, and the absorption band intensity of Hp-C3N4, GO/Hp-C3N4, and GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 at 2900–3600 cm−1 is stronger than that of C3N4, which can be attributed to the introduction of more O-H and N-H groups by the protonation process. Furthermore, in comparison to C3N4, the FI-TR spectra of Hp-C3N4, GO/Hp-C3N4, and GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 reveal a new absorption band at about 610 cm−1, which is associated with the protonation treatment of concentrated HNO3. However, due to the small and uniform distribution of GOQD loading, the typical GOQD absorption band does not show up in the FT-IR spectrum of GOQDs/Hp-C3N4.

The impact of protonation treatment on the chemical makeup of C3N4 and Hp-C3N4 as well as the interaction between Hp-C3N4 and GOQDs was further examined using XPS. According to Fig. 6a, the elements C and N make up the majority of C3N4, with a small amount of O element. After protonation treatment, the O content of Hp-C3N4 and GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 is significantly increased due to the strong oxidation of concentrated HNO3. The characteristic peaks for C-C/CC, N-CN, and CO have binding energies around 284.8, 288.1, and 288.7 eV, respectively, as shown in Fig. 6b. When compared to C3N4, the characteristic peaks of C-C/CC and N-CN of Hp-C3N4 and GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 had smaller relative area proportions, while the characteristic peak of CO had larger relative area proportions. As seen in Fig. 6c, while C3N4 and GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 can be fitted to two peaks, one of which is the C-O group with binding energy at 531.6 eV and the other is the CO group with binding energy at 532.5 eV, the O1s high-resolution spectrum of C3N4 can only be fitted to one characteristic peak of the C-O group at 531.6 eV. This demonstrates that a significant amount of oxygen-containing functional groups is introduced to the surface of Hp-C3N4 and GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 to achieve O doping following protonation of concentrated HNO3.

The N-(C)2, N-(C)3, and N-H groups, are represented by the characteristic peaks near 398.5 eV, 399.4 eV, and 400.8 eV in the high-resolution N 1 s spectra of C3N4, Hp-C3N4, and GOQDs/Hp-C3N4, respectively (see Fig. 6d). In comparison to C3N4, Hp-C3N4's relative area proportions of the N-H characteristic peak at 400.8 eV increase from 6.68% to 39.63%, indicating that more N-H groups are produced on the material's surface by concentrated HNO3 protonation. Furthermore, the N-H of GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 is lower than that of Hp-C3N4 due to amidation between the amino group (N-H) of Hp-C3N4 and the carboxyl and carbonyl groups (CO and COOH) of GOQDs in the electrostatic self-assembly process. The protonation that makes the Hp-C3N4 surface positively charged and causes the localization of the positive charge of the heterocycle also results in a second, broader characteristic peak of Hp-C3N4 at 406.0 eV, which can be correlated to the π-excitation [44]. Due to the mutual attraction between the positive charge on the surface of Hp-C3N4 and the negative charge on the surface of GOQDs during the electrostatic self-assembly process, the positive charge on the surface of GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 is reduced, which is closely related to the findings of the zeta potential analysis. The relative area ratio of this characteristic peak of GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 near 406.0 eV is significantly reduced. This further proves that the electrostatic self-assembly method we used to create the GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 heterojunction was successful.

The UV–vis DRS spectra of C3N4, Hp-C3N4, GO/Hp-C3N4, and GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 are displayed in Fig. 7a. The absorption edge of C3N4 is around 460 nm, which is caused by the n-π * transition (the transition from non-bonding orbital to π * orbital) and the electron transport from N 2p orbital to C 2p orbital within the triazine ring [45]. However, after protonation with concentrated HNO3, the absorption edge of Hp-C3N4 clearly shifts to the blue (424 nm from 460 nm). Similar to the loading of GO and GOQDs, GO/Hp-C3N4 and GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 display the same absorption edge as Hp-C3N4, indicating that GO and GOQDs are only loaded on the surface of Hp-C3N4 through electrostatic self-assembly and are not incorporated into the lattice. Furthermore, in comparison to C3N4, Hp-C3N4, and GO/Hp-C3N4, GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 display noticeably improved absorption and utilization of visible light, which benefits from the effective and broad-spectrum light absorption by GOQDs.

According to Fig. 7b, the band gaps of C3N4, Hp-C3N4, GO/Hp-C3N4, and GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 are 2.811, 3.075, 3.085 and 3.095 eV, respectively. The band gaps of the catalysts were calculated using the tauc plot method. The bandgaps of Hp-C3N4, GO/Hp-C3N4, and GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 are wider than those of C3N4. This can be attributed to two reasons: one is that the protonation treatment exfoliates the bulk C3N4 into small fragmented Hp-C3N4, and the quantum confinement effect caused by the reduction in size and the presence of holes moves the position of the valence band and conduction band, increasing the forbidden bandwidth of Hp-C3N4 [13]; the other is that the protonation introduces a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of Hp-C3N4, including carbonyl, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups. The sp2 covalent bonds of C atoms and N atoms are increasingly replaced by sp3 of O atoms, C atoms, and N atoms, resulting in an increase in the bandgap [46]. In addition, loading GO and GOQDs to construct the heterojunction did not significantly change the bandgap of Hp-C3N4.

Fig. 7c shows the XPS valence band spectra of C3N4 and GOQDs/Hp-C3N4. The valence band positions of C3N4 and GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 are 2.016 eV and 2.367 eV, respectively. Therefore, the conduction band positions of C3N4 and GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 are − 0.795 eV and − 0.708 eV, respectively. Fig. 7d gives the band structures of C3N4 and GOQDs/Hp-C3N4. The conduction band potential required to generate •O2 - is less than − 0.33 eV, and the conduction band potential values of C3N4 and GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 (−0.795 eV and −0.708 eV) are significantly more negative than − 0.33 eV, indicating that both C3N4 and GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 possess sufficient conditions to generate •O2 - [47]. While the valence band potential required to generate •OH is larger than 1.99 eV, the valence band potential of C3N4 (2.016 eV) makes it difficult to generate enough •OH. For GOQDs/Hp-C3N4, its valence band potential is 2.367 eV, which is far more positive than the valence band potential (1.99 eV) required to generate •OH, indicating that GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 could generate abundant •OH more easily in the photocatalytic reaction process [48]. Therefore, the GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 exhibit excellent photocatalytic performance.

The PL spectrum of the catalysts is implied by Fig. 7e. The separation rate of photogenerated electrons and holes in GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 is the fastest. These carriers can quickly migrate to the surface of the catalyst to participate in the photocatalytic reaction and avoid recombination in vivo, according to the sequential decrease in fluorescence intensities of C3N4, Hp-C3N4, GO/Hp-C3N4, and GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 under the excitation wavelength of 300 nm. The PL emission peaks of Hp-C3N4, GO/Hp-C3N4, and GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 also show a distinct blue shift in comparison to C3N4 (emission wavelength = 460 nm), which is in line with UV–vis DRS spectral analysis.

The migration rate of photogenerated charge carriers in the catalyst can be further examined using transient photocurrent response spectroscopy (TPR). Fig. 7f displays the TPR spectra of C3N4, Hp-C3N4, GO/Hp-C3N4, and GOQDs/Hp-C3N4. All catalysts display some photo-response capability under low lighting, but GOQDs/Hp- C3N4 had the strongest transient photocurrent response intensity. The TPR response intensity of GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 is three times higher than that of C3N4, indicating that these materials are more photo responsive and have the capacity to generate more photogenerated electrons and holes.

The photocatalytic performance of the catalysts was evaluated by degrading MO under visible light. The degradation curves of MO by C3N4, Hp-C3N4, GO/Hp-C3N4, and GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 are displayed in Fig. 8a. The adsorption performance of Hp-C3N4, GO/Hp-C3N4, and GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 is superior to that of C3N4, and better adsorption performance could lead to an increase in the number of photocatalytic reaction sites. This suggests that the protonated Hp-C3N4 has a larger specific surface area, which is consistent with the findings of the SEM and BET analysis. Since Hp-C3N4 has a larger specific surface area than C3N4, it can degrade 44% of MO in 105 min while C3N4 has almost no effect on MO degradation. The degradation efficiency of GO/Hp-C3N4 on MO is further improved, and 63% of MO can be degraded in 105 min. The best photocatalytic performance for MO is demonstrated by the electrostatic self-assembled GOQDs/Hp-C3N4, which could degrade 99% of MO at 105 min and even 90% at 60 min. This strongly implies that, when compared to GO, GOQDs are a more suitable cocatalyst to improve the photocatalytic performance of Hp-C3N4. As seen in Fig. 8b, the degradation rate of MO by GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 under visible light is increased by 343 times, 9 times, and 5 times, respectively, in comparison to C3N4, Hp-C3N4, and GO/Hp-C3N4.

The effect of different GOQD loadings on the photocatalytic performance of GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 was investigated. According to Fig. 8c, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 gradually increased as the mass fraction of GOQDs loading increased from 0.5 % to 1.0 %, and when the mass fraction of GOQDs loading reached 1.0 %, the GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 displayed the best photocatalytic performance. As the GOQD loading was increased further, the photocatalytic degradation rate of MO by GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 gradually decreased. This is due to the fact that extra GOQDs will cover the active sites of photocatalytic reactions, restrict light absorption, and serve as a site for photogenerated carriers to recombine, which lowers the catalytic activity of GOQDs/Hp-C3N4.

GOQDs/Hp- C3N4 was used for the cyclic degradation of MO after it was established that it had the best photocatalytic performance, as shown in Fig. 8d. At 105 min into the process, the degradation efficiency of GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 to MO is still higher than 92%. In addition, the XRD patterns of GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 before and after cycling were compared. The XRD patterns of GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 after cyclic degradation of MO do not change significantly. Both only have a diffraction peak at 2θ = 27.48°, which corresponds to the interlayer stacking of the (002) plane (see Fig. 8e), indicating that the cyclic degradation of MO does not change the crystal structure of GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 [49]. According to these results, our GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 heterojunctions are well-prepared and have high photocatalytic activity and cycling stability [50,51].

By using radical trapping experiments, the primary reactive species in the photocatalytic degradation of MO by GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 were investigated. Sodium oxalate (Na2C2O4), isopropanol (IPQ), and p-benzoquinone (PBQ) were used as scavengers for photogenerated holes (h+), hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and superoxide radicals (•O2-), respectively. The influence of various scavengers on MO degradation by GOQDs/Hp- C3N4 is depicted in Fig. 9a. As can be seen, the addition of Na2C2O4, IPQ, and PBQ all inhibit MO degradation by GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 to varying degrees. Fig. 9b shows that with the addition of Na2C2O4, IPQ, and PBQ, the degradation efficiencies of GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 for MO at 105 min are 67 %, 38 %, and 32 %, respectively. This indicates that the effect of reactive species on the degradation of MO by GOQDs/Hp- C3N4 is: •OH＞•O2 -＞h+.

To further confirm the reactive radicals produced by the GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 in the photocatalytic reaction, ESR tests were carried out using DMPO as a spin trap. As shown in Fig. 9c-d, the characteristic peaks of •O2 - and •OH adducts are not detected under dark conditions for C3N4, GO/Hp-C3N4, and GOQDs/Hp-C3N4, indicating that none of the catalysts could produce •O2 - and •OH in the dark. The characteristic peaks of •O2 - and •OH adducts are detected by C3N4, GO/Hp-C3N4, and GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 after an 8-minute illumination period, and the signal intensity increases in turn. In particular, the intensity of the characteristic peaks of C3N4 for •O2 - and •OH is weak, suggesting that C3N4 is difficult to generate •O2 - and •OH (especially for •OH) under the light conditions, which is consistent with the previous energy band structure analysis. The intensity of the characteristic peaks of GO/Hp-C3N4 about •O2 - and •OH is improved compared to C3N4, which is related to the protonation treatment and the loading of GO. Compared with C3N4 and GO/Hp-C3N4, the signal intensity of the characteristic peaks of GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 about •O2 - and •OH are significantly increased, further confirming that •O2 - and •OH are the main factors in the photocatalytic degradation of MO by GOQDs/Hp-C3N4. It also indicates that GOQD is a more ideal co-catalyst than that of GO to construct GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 heterojunctions with efficient photocatalytic performance with Hp-C3N4.

It is well-studied that C3N4 has poor photocatalytic performance in visible light due to the disadvantages of having a small specific surface area, weak visible light absorption, and rapid complexation of photogenerated carriers. In order to build a GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 heterojunction, as shown in Fig. 10 , protonation and electrostatic self-assembly with GOQDs are required. During the protonation process, bulk C3N4 is converted into fragmented porous Hp-C3N4, which increases the photocatalytic reaction site (specific surface area) and decreases the migration distance of photogenerated carriers. The concentrated HNO3 protonation process, in the meantime, accomplishes O element doping and the introduction of oxygen-containing defects to adjust the energy band structure of the catalyst, which permits the valence band potential of GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 to generate enough •OH.

More importantly, because GOQDs are abundant in oxygen-containing functional groups and are extremely small on the nanoscale, they can disperse evenly with Hp-C3N4 and form good, stable interfacial bonds. This makes it possible for GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 to produce more photogenerated carriers and encourage them to move quickly to the surface where they can take part in photocatalytic redox reactions rather than recombination. Additionally, it enables the full utilization of GOQDs' superior electron transfer capability, efficient broad-spectrum absorption, and unique photo-induced up-conversion effect. In contrast to C3N4, Hp-C3N4, and GO/GOQDs, GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 eventually produce more oxidation-capable photocatalytic reaction active substances like •OH, •O2 -, and h+, leading to the best photocatalytic performance of GOQDs. Hence, the organic pollutant macromolecule MO is degraded into H2O and CO2 through a series of redox reactions.

After splitting GO with concentrated HNO3 to produce GOQDs with excellent performance, and after treating C3N4 with concentrated HNO3 to produce Hp-C3N4, it was then possible to create GO/Hp-C3N4 heterojunctions and GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 heterojunctions using an electrostatic self-assembly method. The GOQDs are more ideal cocatalysts than GO because of their smaller sizes and abundance of surface functional groups containing oxygen. The as-prepared GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 simultaneously modified the morphology of C3N4, tuned the energy band (O element doping), and built heterojunctions, exhibiting highly effective photocatalytic performance. The rate of MO degradation by GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 under visible light was increased by 343 times, 9 times, and 5 times, respectively, in comparison to C3N4, Hp-C3N4, and GO/Hp-C3N4. Our developed GOQDs/Hp-C3N4 not only offer an effective and environmentally friendly method for the photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants, but they also offer a cutting-edge method for using graphene-based nanomaterials in carbon nitride-based photocatalysts.
